explanation: Tribehenin is a glyceryl triester of behenic acid (C22 fatty acid) used as a smooth, elegant emollient and structuring wax in lip products and anhydrous formulas, providing a non-greasy, waxy texture. It is considered well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Tricaprylin (glyceryl tricaprylate) is a saturated triglyceride from coconut/palm kernel-derived caprylic acid (C8), used as an ultra-light, fast-absorbing emollient with a dry, non-greasy skin feel and excellent oxidative stability. It is well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and used in lightweight moisturizers and sunscreens for its comfortable, shelf-stable skin feel.

Triclosan is a broad-spectrum chlorinated phenolic antimicrobial banned by the FDA from over-the-counter antiseptic wash products (2017) due to concerns about endocrine disruption (thyroid hormone interference), contact sensitization, and promotion of antibiotic resistance, without demonstrated superiority over soap and water. It is flagged as a sensitizer and is progressively being phased out of cosmetic formulations.

Tridecane is a linear C13 saturated hydrocarbon emollient used as a lightweight, fast-absorbing carrier in sunscreens and serums, providing an ultra-dry, non-greasy skin feel. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated, functioning as a sensory modifier and carrier rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Trideceth-10 is an ethoxylated tridecyl alcohol (10 moles EO) with predominantly hydrophilic character, used as a mild emulsifier and solubilizer in oil-in-water emulsions. It is generally well-tolerated at typical cosmetic use concentrations; individuals with PEG sensitivity should note the ethoxylate modification.

Trideceth-6 is an ethoxylated tridecyl alcohol (6 moles EO) with more balanced hydrophilic-lipophilic character than trideceth-10, used as a mild emulsifier in skincare and cleansing formulas. It is generally well-tolerated; individuals with PEG sensitivity should note the ethoxylate modification.

Tridecyl trimellitate is a tridecyl ester of trimellitic acid used as a non-greasy, oxidatively stable emollient in color cosmetics and sunscreens, providing good spreadability and comfortable skin adhesion. It is considered well-tolerated and non-comedogenic at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Triethanolamine (TEA) is an amine-based pH adjuster and emulsifier widely used to neutralize acidic ingredients and bring formulas to the correct skin-compatible pH. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic concentrations; EU regulations limit concentration due to N-nitrosamine formation concerns, and frequent high-level use on compromised skin is not recommended.

Triethanolamine lauryl sulfate is the triethanolamine salt of lauryl sulfate — an anionic sulfate surfactant with a similar irritation profile to sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS) — providing powerful cleansing with well-documented primary skin irritation potential. It is flagged as a sulfate surfactant; individuals with sensitive, reactive, or eczema-prone skin should prefer non-sulfate cleansing alternatives.

Triethoxycaprylyl silane is a silane coupling agent used to hydrophobically coat mineral pigments (titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, iron oxides), improving their dispersibility, reducing migration, and enhancing long-wear performance in cosmetics. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing, functioning as a functional pigment surface treatment rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Triethoxycaprylylsilane — an alternate spelling of triethoxycaprylyl silane — is the same silane coupling agent used to hydrophobically coat mineral pigments (titanium dioxide, zinc oxide) to improve dispersibility and long-wear performance in cosmetics. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic concentrations.

Triethyl citrate is a triester of citric acid and ethanol used in cosmetics as a mild emollient and deodorant active that inhibits bacterial lipase — the enzyme responsible for odor-producing fatty acid cleavage — without the endocrine concerns of triclosan or aluminum salts. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic concentrations.

Triethylene glycol is a short-chain glycol ether used as a mild humectant, co-solvent, and antimicrobial agent in skincare formulas. It is generally well-tolerated at typical cosmetic use concentrations, functioning as a humectant and formula modifier rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Triethylhexanoin is a glyceryl triester of 2-ethylhexanoic acid used as a lightweight, oxidatively stable emollient with a dry, non-greasy skin feel and excellent spreadability in sunscreens and color cosmetics. It is considered well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Trihydroxystearin is a hydroxylated castor oil-derived glyceryl ester used as a thickener, texturizer, and emollient in anhydrous formulas and color cosmetics, providing a smooth, waxy structure and good pigment dispersion. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Trimethylsiloxysilicate is a crosslinked silicone resin used as a long-wear film-former in foundations, lipsticks, and eyeliners, providing exceptional wear durability, transfer resistance, and water resistance without occlusion. It is considered well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and non-comedogenic at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Tripeptide-1 (glycyl-histidyl-lysine, GHK) is a signal tripeptide that functions as a copper-chelating precursor with documented collagen-stimulating, wound-healing, and anti-inflammatory activity at nanomolar concentrations. It is well-tolerated, non-irritating, and used in anti-aging and firming formulas for its copper-peptide complex and matrix-stimulating properties.

Trisiloxane is a low-viscosity volatile silicone (MD₂M structure) used as a lightweight emollient and spreading agent that evaporates from the skin surface leaving a smooth, non-greasy finish. It is considered well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and non-comedogenic, functioning as a carrier and sensory modifier.

Trisodium ethylenediamine disuccinate (EDDS) is a biodegradable amino acid-derived chelating agent that sequesters metal ions to prevent oxidative formula degradation and enhance preservative efficacy. It is considered well-tolerated, readily biodegradable, and preferred in natural and green-chemistry formulas over conventional EDTA.

Triticum vulgare (wheat) germ oil is one of the richest natural sources of vitamin E (tocopherol) alongside linoleic acid and phytosterols, providing antioxidant, barrier-supporting, and nourishing emollient properties. It is well-tolerated by most skin types; individuals with severe wheat allergies should note the wheat origin, though topically applied wheat proteins present minimal systemic risk on intact skin.

Triticum vulgare (wheat) seed extract provides tocopherols, ferulic acid, phytosterols, and polyphenol antioxidants with free-radical-scavenging and barrier-supporting properties. It is well-tolerated and used in protective and antioxidant formulas for its vitamin E and ferulic acid antioxidant content.

Tromethamine (TRIS, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane) is an organic amine used as a pH adjuster and buffer in cosmetic formulas to maintain stable, skin-compatible pH. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations, functioning as a pH stabilizer rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Ubiquinone (Coenzyme Q10, CoQ10) is a naturally occurring lipid-soluble antioxidant in human cells that declines with age, used in skincare for its potent free-radical-scavenging activity in the cellular lipid environment and its documented ability to reduce the appearance of fine lines. It is well-tolerated, non-irritating, and used in anti-aging and protective formulas for its cellular antioxidant and anti-aging activity.

Ulmus davidiana (David elm) root extract provides polyphenols and mucilaginous compounds with anti-inflammatory, soothing, and skin-conditioning properties, widely used in K-beauty formulations. It is well-tolerated and used in calming and protective formulas for its anti-inflammatory and skin-soothing content.

Undaria pinnatifida (wakame) seaweed extract provides fucoidan, fucoxanthin, alginates, and carotenoid antioxidants with anti-inflammatory, hydrating, and UV-protective properties. It is well-tolerated and used in anti-aging and protective formulas for its marine polysaccharide and antioxidant content.

Undecane is a linear C11 saturated hydrocarbon emollient used as a lightweight, fast-absorbing, low-viscosity carrier in sunscreens and serums, providing an ultra-dry skin feel without a greasy residue. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated, functioning as a carrier rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Undecylenoyl glycine is a fatty acid-amino acid conjugate combining undecylenic acid (from castor oil) and glycine, used as a mild, natural-positioned antimicrobial and preservative active particularly effective against Candida and Cutibacterium acnes. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic concentrations, used in natural and clean-formula preservative systems.

Urea is a naturally occurring compound in the skin's NMF (natural moisturizing factor), used both as a potent humectant at low concentrations (≤10%) and as a keratolytic exfoliant at higher concentrations (≥20%) that promotes shedding of dry, thickened, or hyperkeratotic skin. It is generally well-tolerated but may cause temporary stinging on highly sensitive, broken, or severely inflamed skin.

Urtica dioica (stinging nettle) leaf extract provides flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol), vitamins A and C, chlorophyll, and polyphenol antioxidants with anti-inflammatory and free-radical-scavenging properties. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in protective and anti-inflammatory formulas for its antioxidant content.

Urtica dioica leaf extract is stinging nettle extract providing flavonoids and polyphenol antioxidants — the same ingredient as Urtica Dioica (Nettle) Leaf Extract, listed without the common name parenthetical. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic extract concentrations and used in protective and anti-inflammatory formulas.
